text: Robotic repair of a recurrent lumbar hernia. The objective of this presentation is to provide the learner with the operative approach and technique for robotic repair of a recurrent lumbar hernia.

Lumbar hernias can form in either the inferior lumbar triangle, which lies superficially, or the superior lumbar triangle, which is deep. Of the two, the superior triangle is more commonly the site of herniation.

The superior lumbar triangle is formed medially by the sacrospinalis, laterally by the posterior border of the internal abdominal oblique, and superiorly by the twelfth rib. The floor of the superior lumbar triangle is the transversalis fascia, and its roof is the external abdominal oblique.

The video shows the anatomical boundaries of the inferior lumbar triangle, defined by the iliac crest, latissimus dorsi, and external abdominal oblique, with the internal abdominal oblique forming the floor.

Our patient is a fifty-five year old woman with a BMI of twenty-one point three. who presented with a recurrent left posterior bulge. She reported that she had a posterior lumbar hernia repair in in that did not involve mesh. The hernia recurred shortly thereafter, but she did not seek treatment for several years. However, it started to enlarge and become more symptomatic, so she has now requested surgical repair.
The preoperative abdominal CT scan reveals a left lumbar hernia measuring 2.7 by 2.5 centimeters, containing fat.
The patient is positioned in the steep reverse Trendelenburg position, lying on her right side in the lateral decubitus position with her arms extended outward.
The surgeon inserts a 12mm trocar in the left upper quadrant using the Hassan technique, positioning it lateral to the semilunar line and 2-3 centimeters below the costal margin.
The surgeon insufflates the abdomen and inserts an eight millimeter robotic camera with a thirty-degree angle. Three additional eight millimeter ports are placed for the robotic instruments.
The surgeon inserts trocars along the left hemiabdomen to enhance visualization and facilitate adhesiolysis.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum three centimeters lateral to the colonic reflection and continues dissection through the retroperitoneal fat, revealing the transversus abdominis muscle.

The surgeon carefully dissects within the avascular plane, extending the dissection cranially onto the diaphragm by six to seven centimeters and caudally into the pelvis past the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).
The surgeon continues the dissection medially, exposing the quadratus lumborum and psoas muscle, while clearly identifying and preserving the subcostal nerve.
The retroperitoneal fat is visibly herniating through the posterior lumbar defect, with the edges of the hernia defect clearly defined.
The surgeon cuts and removes a stitch from a prior repair, revealing a hernia defect measuring three by two centimeters.
The surgeon attempts to close the posterior lumbar defect using a 1-0 V-Loc suture, but the suture pulls through the tissue due to its friable nature.

A seven and a half by fifteen centimeter barred soft mesh is placed into the abdominal cavity, centered over the hernia defect with overlap in each direction. The mesh is secured using a 2-0 Vicryl stitch.
The surgeon inspects the mesh to confirm it lies completely flat within the posterior space, ensuring proper placement over the hernia defect.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum with a running two-oh V-lock suture, covering the previously exposed area.
The final visualization shows the completed repair with the mesh lying flat and secured over the defect in the abdominal cavity.
One month after surgery, the patient reported no issues and CT scan showed no evidence of recurrent hernia.
